does: This paper decomposes GPT-2 XL and Llama-3.1-8B using sparse autoencoders to extract 16K-32K interpretable features per layer, then validates these features against human brain recordings during language processing. The authors create a human-validated taxonomy of semantic features and demonstrate that semantic features alone recover 94% of peak encoding performance in predicting brain responses. They further test whether five semantic subcategories derived from neuroscience map onto distinct cortical regions, finding significant alignment (Spearman ρ=0.72, p<0.001) that confirms LLM features recapitulate known cortical semantic organization at unprecedented granularity.
connects: This work bridges mechanistic interpretability and computational neurolinguistics, building on the robust finding that intermediate LLM layers predict human brain responses to language better than other layers. It extends prior neural encoding models that have mapped LLM representations to brain activity by replacing opaque layer activations with interpretable sparse autoencoder features. The approach combines recent advances in SAE-based feature discovery—which decompose neural networks into human-interpretable dimensions—with established neuroscience frameworks of cortical semantic organization, particularly work on semantic topography across brain regions.
enables: This work establishes a methodology for directly comparing mechanistically interpretable AI representations with brain organization, opening new directions for understanding both LLMs and neurobiology. By demonstrating that SAE features align with known cortical structure, it suggests SAEs could serve as tools for neuroscience hypothesis generation and validation. The framework enables future work to map other cognitive processes onto brain topography, predict how different architectures reflect neural organization, and potentially use brain data to constrain or improve LLM interpretability methods. This alignment also suggests that semantic organization principles discovered in neuroscience may be fundamental to language models.
